title: Describe a time you had to dive deep to debug a hard production issue under pressure.

transcript.text: Describe a time you had to dive deep to debug a hard production issue under pressure.

blocks.md:
## Dive deep to debug a hard production issue under pressure — no company named, so I'll frame it as a strong-tech HM round with an Amazon "Dive Deep" lean
Story: W1 — the 5-day silent Kafka failure · Dive Deep · ~110s

### Situation
At Walmart, our audit-logging pipeline just... stopped writing data. Kafka Connect into a GCS sink. The bad part — nothing errored. No alert, no exception, dashboards all green. It took us five days to even realize data was missing, because we had no consumer-lag monitoring at the time. And this is supplier-facing audit data for accounts like Pepsi, so it mattered.

### Task
I owned the pipeline, so I owned the root cause. I had to figure out why a system that reported perfectly healthy was silently losing data — and make sure it could never go quiet like that again.

### Action
I didn't trust the "everything's fine" signal, so I started from the data and walked backward. It was actually a five-link chain.

A null SMT header was causing a `SinkRecord` NPE deep in the connector. Connect caught that and silently retried instead of failing loud. Because it kept stalling, KEDA's poll-timeout autoscaler misread the stall as high load and scaled up more workers. Those extra workers drove the JVM heap into OOM. So every layer was "handling" the problem by hiding it.

> 💬 Plain-English version to say out loud: "One bad message broke a worker quietly. The system, instead of shouting, kept retrying and even added more workers — so it looked busy and healthy while actually doing nothing."

The fix itself was small — an SMT null-guard plus bounded backoff so a bad record couldn't trigger that retry storm. But the real work was the guardrails. I added a Micrometer counter on null headers, a Grafana alert at 0.1%, and a CI chaos test that injects a bad header so this exact failure gets caught before prod.

### Result
Zero data loss — we replayed from Kafka and recovered everything. Then I added two-tier consumer-lag alerting, warning at 100 and critical at 500. That alerting has since caught two issues before they became incidents.

> 💬 The line I'd land on: "'Is it running?' is not monitoring. 'Is it processing correctly without losing data?' — that's monitoring. That's the lesson I took from those five days."

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- **SMT = Single Message Transform** — a small transform Kafka Connect runs on each record. A null header field hit a code path that threw an NPE building the `SinkRecord`.
- **Silent retry**: Connect's default behavior retried the failing task rather than dead-lettering or failing the connector, so no error propagated up.
- **KEDA** scales workers off a metric — here poll-timeout. A stalled consumer looks like lag, so it scaled *up*, which is exactly wrong for this failure.
- **OOM**: more workers, same heap ceiling → JVM heap exhausted. That was the only symptom that eventually showed, and it was a downstream effect, not the cause.
- **Guardrails**: null-header counter + 0.1% Grafana alert + a CI chaos test injecting malformed headers, plus lag alerts at 100/500.

**Likely follow-ups:**
- *Why did it take 5 days?* → No consumer-lag monitoring and every layer swallowed the error. First real signal was the heap OOM, which is 4 links down the chain.
- *How did you confirm zero data loss?* → Kafka retained the records; we replayed from the offset and reconciled counts in BigQuery.
- *Why 0.1% as the alert threshold?* → Null headers should basically never happen, so anything sustained above noise means a real upstream change.
- *Why chaos-test it in CI?* → A unit test with clean data would never reproduce it. The bug only exists with malformed input, so I inject malformed input.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't make it sound like the fix was the hero — the *diagnosis* and the *guardrails* are the story. The null-guard is one line.
- Don't blame KEDA or Connect as "broken." They behaved as configured; the gap was ours — no lag monitoring.
- Don't claim we caught it fast. Own the 5 days honestly; that's the whole point of the lesson.

🎙️ **Say-it script:**
"So at Walmart our audit-logging pipeline just stopped writing data — Kafka Connect into a GCS sink. The scary part was it was silent. No error, dashboards green, and it took us five days to even notice, because we had no consumer-lag monitoring. And this is supplier-facing data, so it mattered.

I owned the pipeline, so I dug in from the data backward. Turned out to be a five-link chain. A null header broke a transform with an NPE. Connect quietly retried instead of failing loud. KEDA saw the stall, thought it was load, and scaled up more workers. And those workers drove the heap into OOM. Every layer was hiding the problem.

The fix was small — a null-guard and bounded backoff. But I spent the real effort on guardrails: a counter on null headers, a Grafana alert at 0.1%, and a CI chaos test that injects a bad header so this can't come back. We replayed from Kafka, zero data loss. Then I added lag alerting at 100 and 500, and that's already caught two issues early. The lesson — 'is it running' isn't monitoring. 'Is it processing correctly without losing data' is."

💬 **60-second version:**
"Our audit pipeline — Kafka Connect to GCS — silently stopped writing for five days. No errors, all green. I traced a five-link chain: a null header threw an NPE, Connect retried quietly, KEDA misread the stall and scaled up, and that OOM'd the heap. The fix was a small null-guard, but the real work was guardrails — a null-header counter, a 0.1% alert, a CI chaos test, and lag alerting at 100/500. Zero data loss after replay. My takeaway: 'is it running' isn't monitoring — 'is it processing correctly' is."
